title: Pathak 등 - 2022 - An Adaptive QoS and Trust-Based Lightweight Secure Routing Algorithm for WSNs

sections.name: Body
sections.content:
23826 IEEE INTERNET OF THINGS JOURNAL, VOL. 9, NO. 23, 1 DECEMBER 2022
An Adaptive QoS and Trust-Based Lightweight
Secure Routing Algorithm for WSNs
Aditya Pathak , Graduate Student Member, IEEE , Irfan Al-Anbagi , Senior Member, IEEE,
and Howard J. Hamilton
Abstract—The limited resources and low computational power
of wireless sensor networks (WSNs) make them vulnerable to various security attacks. Conventional security mechanisms require
too many resources to allow the reliable operation of WSNs due to
their resource-constrained nature. In addition, multihop communication in WSNs creates a requirement for guaranteed Quality
of Service (QoS). Therefore, providing security while maintaining QoS and energy efﬁciency in WSNs are important design
considerations. To further increase the performance of WSNs,
there is a need to overcome the energy-hole problem, which
leads to poor coverage of the ﬁeld of interest. An energy-hole
problem is created because of using poor deployment strategies.
In this article, we deﬁne a multiobjective WSN optimization
problem and present a novel algorithm known as lightweight
secure routing (LSR) to manage WSNs that directly addresses the
multiobjective WSN optimization problem. Our LSR algorithm
uses ant colony optimization (ACO), an adaptive security model
based on direct and indirect trust calculations, an adaptive QoS
model, a hybrid deployment model based on 2-D Gaussian and
uniform distributions, and an adaptive connectivity model that
uses an appropriate communicational radius to ensure high connectivity between sensor nodes to solve the multiobjective WSN
optimization problem. We divide our simulation results into three
analyses, namely, trust model analysis, network scalability analysis, and security risk analysis to show that LSR outperforms
the existing techniques in terms of energy consumed to calculate
trust values, trust values convergence, network lifetime, average
routing delay, and packet delivery ratio.
Index Terms —Ant colony optimization (ACO), energy efﬁciency, energy-hole problem, lightweight security, multiobjective
wireless sensor network (WSN) optimization problem, network
security, Quality of Service (QoS), trust model, WSNs.
I. I NTRODUCTION
T
HE DYNAMIC nature and simple operation of wireless sensor networks (WSNs) make them an attractive
choice for many Internet of Things (IoT) applications. The
number of sensors deployed in WSNs increased from 0.45 to
2 billion between 2012 and 2020 [ 1]. However, the simplicity
Manuscript received 2 June 2022; accepted 30 June 2022. Date of publication 11 July 2022; date of current version 21 November 2022. This work was
supported by ISM Canada. The work of Howard J. Hamilton was supported
by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada through
the Collaborative Research and Development Grant. (Corresponding author:
Irfan Al-Anbagi.)
Aditya Pathak and Irfan Al-Anbagi are with the Faculty of Engineering and
Applied Science, University of Regina, Regina, SK S4S 0A2, Canada (e-mail:
apa142@uregina.ca; irfan.al-anbagi@uregina.ca).
Howard J. Hamilton is with the Department of Computer Science,
University of Regina, Regina, SK S4S 0A2, Canada (e-mail: hamilton@
cs.uregina.ca).
Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/JIOT.2022.3189832
of WSNs limits the available resources in the nodes, which
imposes challenges for algorithm design and WSN security.
Due to these design challenges, existing WSNs are vulnerable
to security attacks. First, the wireless nature of communication allows eavesdropping on trafﬁc exchanged in the WSN,
making the nodes prone to several types of security attacks.
Second, the sensor nodes have limited computing capabilities,
limited communication bandwidth, and low storage capacity
due to their small size and limited energy. Third, in remote
WSN applications, such as environmental monitoring, healthcare monitoring, and battleﬁeld surveillance, the nodes can be
easily captured and compromised, which threatens the viability of their cryptography-based security algorithms. Overall,
security is a crucial challenge for current WSNs because disruptive and malicious actions are able to compromise their
functioning to a signiﬁcant extent [ 1]–[3].
Due to the above limitations, it is not easy to directly
employ conventional cryptography-based security solutions in
WSNs [1]. Most conventional security models defend against
external attacks but not internal ones. Since the network cannot differentiate between compromised and legitimate nodes,
network performance, such as routing delay, network lifetime,
etc, are degraded [ 3]. In past, key management algorithms [ 4]
and user authentication schemes [3] have been used to achieve
security in WSNs. However, they are ineffective against
internal attacks and require more computation to secure WSNs.
Thus, energy efﬁciency decreases and sometimes Quality of
Service (QoS) constraints, such as minimal delay (required to
support real-time operation) are not achieved. Overall, security is increased at the expense of higher energy consumption
and longer delays. This type of tradeoff is not suitable for
resource-constrained networks like WSNs. Therefore, security
models that mitigate internal attacks and provide energyefﬁcient operation while satisfying QoS constraints are needed
for WSNs [ 2].
One approach to detect and defend against internal attacks,
and also satisfy the above requirements of energy efﬁciency
and QoS, is to base security in a WSN on a trust model [ 5].
A trust model speciﬁes how a node can evaluate the trustworthiness of other nodes, using information present at the node
(direct trust) or gathered from other nodes ( indirect trust)[ 6].
Trust models with direct and indirect trust calculations provide
higher security than those with only direct trust calculations
because indirect trust methods can isolate the activity from
compromised nodes much faster than direct trust methods and
select secure routes [ 7]. However, calculating indirect trust

PATHAK et al.: ADAPTIVE QoS AND TRUST-BASED LIGHTWEIGHT SECURE ROUTING ALGORITHM FOR WSNs 23827
values in WSNs is an energy-consuming process, and the
resource constraints of WSNs preclude the calculation of high
levels of indirect trust. Therefore, to provide security in an
energy-efﬁcient manner, a trust model should be lightweight,
i.e., it should require few calculations.
In addition to the above challenges, nodes near the base
station (BS) of the WSN consume their energy much faster
than other nodes because these nodes relay all the trafﬁc from
the other nodes to BS. This is known as the energy-hole
problem [8]. The energy-hole problem imposes fundamental
design constraints on WSNs, including a tradeoff between coverage and network lifetime. Coverage refers to the fraction of
the monitored area that is accurately represented by the collected data. Network lifetime refers to the length of time (in
rounds) for which the WSN is effective at collecting sensor
data [8]. Here, a round is the interval during which an event
is detected and the event data is transmitted from the source
node to BS. The energy-hole problem can cause BS to become
detached from the remainder of the WSN, which reduces the
network’s lifetime, QoS, and security.
To promote the widespread adoption of secure WSNs,
multiple objectives must be addressed. The multiobjective
WSN optimization problem is the problem of designing a
WSN that mitigates the energy-hole problem, provides high
coverage, and jointly optimizes energy efﬁciency, QoS, and
security. This article is the ﬁrst to formulate this multiobjective
problem.
To solve this problem, we propose a lightweight secure routing (LSR) algorithm, a novel algorithm for managing WSNs
that directly addresses the multiobjective WSN optimization
problem. Our algorithm achieves that by integrating four new
models. First, our adaptive QoS model enhances QoS and
energy efﬁciency by improving the selection of the source
node. Second, our adaptive security model improves security
using a trust model that calculates direct and indirect trust
values. Third, our adaptive connectivity model mitigates the
energy-hole problem in low node density networks by improving the connectivity between sensor nodes. Finally, our hybrid
deployment model uses the Gaussian and uniform distribution
deployment to mitigate the energy-hole problem for high node
density networks and provides high coverage. The LSR algorithm does not negatively affect the network lifetime of sensor
nodes. We divide our simulation results into three analyses,
namely, trust model analysis, network scalability analysis, and
security risk analysis, to show that LSR outperforms existing techniques in terms of energy consumption for calculating
trust values, trust value convergence, network lifetime, average
routing delay, and packet delivery ratio.
Our main novel contributions are summarized as follows.
1) We propose an adaptive QoS model to enhance QoS
and energy efﬁciency of the network by improving the
selection of a source node. We measure QoS using average routing delay and PDR, and energy efﬁciency using
network lifetime.
2) We propose an adaptive security model to improve
network security by incorporating a trust model. We
measure security using average routing delay and
PDR. Also, we measure the energy consumption for
calculating trust values as well as the rate of convergence for the trust values in order to provide evidence
that our trust model is lightweight and secure.
3) We propose an adaptive connectivity model that mitigates the energy-hole problem for low node density
networks by improving connectivity between sensor
nodes. We measure the performance of this model using
network lifetime, average routing delay, and PDR.
4) We propose a hybrid deployment model that improves
coverage in WSNs and mitigates the energy-hole
problem for high node density networks. We measure
the performance of this model using network lifetime,
average routing delay, and PDR.
The remainder of this article is organized as follows. In
Section II, we summarize related work. In Section III, we discuss our network models and assumptions. In Section IV , we
introduce the LSR algorithm. In Section V , we present simulation results and analysis. Finally, in Section VI, we conclude
this article.
II. R
ELATED WORK
Security in a WSN is achieved either by data encryption and
key management or by identifying and removing compromised
nodes. Abdmeziem and Tandjaoui [ 4] have proposed a key
management algorithm for providing end-to-end security for ehealth applications. Liu and Chung [ 3] have proposed a secure
user authentication scheme for WSNs. However, to establish
secure communications, we need to ensure that all communicating nodes are trusted. Thus, it is critical to choose a trust
model that allows a sensor node to infer the trustworthiness
of another node.
Dhanalakshmi et al. [9] have proposed a secure and energyefﬁcient routing protocol for WSNs using fuzzy logic and
node’s trust values to improve the packet delivery ratio. The
protocol can address challenges, such as node mobility, energy
balancing, and energy optimization by predicting an optimal
communication path in the network. Fang et al. [10]h a v e
proposed a Gaussian distribution-based comprehensive trust
management system (GDTMS) for industrial WSNs. Greybased decision-making theory selects the most reliable packet
forwarding neighbor node based on trust values and the number of hops as input. The simulation results show that GDTMS
achieves a good tradeoff between security, energy, and transmission performance (throughput). Rodrigues and John [ 11]
have proposed a trust-based routing algorithm named Joint
Trust to ﬁnd a secure routing path using parameters, such as
path integrity factor, path consistency factor, forwarding rate,
and path availability factor. A ChickenDragonﬂy (CHicDra)
optimization algorithm is used to ﬁnd optimal cluster heads
for secure communication.
Ademaj and Bernhard [ 12] have proposed a QoS-based
minimal routing protocol for WSNs using a time-division
multiple access scheme. The routing protocol can minimize
end-to-end delay, provide balanced power consumption, and
improve energy efﬁciency by avoiding packet collisions in
the network. Abuzainab et al. [13] have proposed a QoS
Authorized licensed use limited to: University of Regina IEL. Downloaded on June 16,2025 at 15:34:17 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply.

23828 IEEE INTERNET OF THINGS JOURNAL, VOL. 9, NO. 23, 1 DECEMBER 2022
and jamming-aware routing protocol to ensure robust communication against eavesdropping and jamming attacks using
deep reinforcement learning. The protocol can maximize the
network performance in terms of QoS, security, and energy
efﬁciency. Rastogi et al. [14] have proposed one such hybrid
optimization algorithm that uses ant colony optimization
(ACO) and particle swarm optimization (PSO) to provide reliable routing, which enhances the network lifetime of WSNs.
However, the algorithm does not consider the effect of compromised nodes and no security parameter is involved in the
calculation of routing paths.
Li and Song [ 15] have proposed an attack-resistant trust
management algorithm to cope with malicious vehicles in
vehicular networks. The trustworthiness of a vehicle is calculated based on functional trust and recommendation trust.
Zhang et al. [16] have proposed a trust management algorithm
using artiﬁcial intelligence (AI) and the blockchain technology
to address security challenges in vehicular networks. A deep
learning algorithm is used to establish and manage the trust
values of vehicles, and the blockchain technology is used to
validate the identities of the vehicles in the network. However,
both algorithms ignore QoS considerations, which are highly
relevant to vehicular networks.
Existing WSN designs have varying levels of effectiveness
with respect to the energy-hole problem. Mohemed et al. [8]
have proposed two routing protocols known as on-hole children reconnection (OHCR) and on-hole alert (OHA) to mitigate the energy-hole problem in WSNs. Lipare et al. [17]
have proposed an energy-efﬁcient routing structure using
a multilayer network model consisting of the clustering
scheme to mitigate the energy-hole problem around BS.
Roselin et al. [18] have proposed an energy-efﬁcient connected coverage (EECC) scheduling to increase the network
lifetime and mitigate the energy-hole problem. Previous methods [8], [17], [18] for solving the energy-hole problem require
extensive computation on the sensor nodes and do not consider the effect of compromised nodes in their network.
For instance, a ﬂooding attack can decrease the energy of
nearby nodes giving rise to an energy-hole problem. Thus,
it proves ineffective for WSNs scenarios. One promising solution to the energy-hole problem is to deploy more sensors
close to BS, which is possible using the Gaussian distribution deployment method, with BS as the center point [ 21].
However, this method decreases the coverage area of sensor nodes in the Field of Interest (FoI). Luo et al. [19]
have proposed an algorithm to increase the coverage area
in FoI known as the maximum coverage sets scheduling
(MCSS) algorithm using a greedy approach. However, the
authors do not consider the effect of compromised nodes
in the network and do not solve the energy-hole problem.
Therefore, it is important to mitigate the energy-hole problem,
increase the coverage area in the FoI, and consider the
effect of compromised nodes in the network at the same
time.
Rathee et al. [20] have proposed the QoS-aware energy balancing secure routing (QEBSR) algorithm, which uses ACO.
QEBSR currently provides a good solution to the problem
of simultaneous optimization of energy efﬁciency, QoS, and
security. However, four shortcomings should be addressed to
improve the network’s effectiveness.
1) The effects of energy-hole and coverage problems are
not addressed because of QEBSR’s uniform deployment
model. It results in a low network lifetime.
2) The trust model is limited to direct trust values, which
reduces the mitigation of compromised nodes and
increases energy consumption in the network due to frequent packet drops and retransmissions. It results in a
lower network lifetime and compromised security in the
network.
3) There is unbalanced energy consumption at the nodes
during the process of selecting the source node. This
results in higher energy consumption for a few nodes
and ultimately reduces the network lifetime and QoS.
4) The QEBSR algorithm has not been validated using
a network simulator, making it difﬁcult to analyze its
performance when deployed in a real environment.
According to our related work (summarized in Table I),
previous researchers have addressed various parts of the
multiobjective WSN optimization problem. However, to
improve the performance of WSNs signiﬁcantly, one needs to
solve the entire multiobjective WSN optimization problem as a
whole, i.e., the algorithm must be able to mitigate the energyhole problem, provide high coverage, and jointly optimize
energy efﬁciency, QoS, and security. In addition, the approach
of Rathee et al. [20], while promising, has the four shortcomings listed above. Therefore, in this article, we address
these four shortcomings and also provide a novel and effective
solution to the multiobjective WSN optimization problem.
III. N
ETWORK MODELS AND ASSUMPTIONS
In this section, we introduce our network models and
assumptions.
A. WSN Deployment Model
We consider a WSN network in a 2-D plane that consists of
a BS node and N homogeneous sensor nodes in a square FoI
with side length L and area A = L2. We assume that the nodes
of a WSN are randomly deployed in the FoI, e.g., by dropping
from an airplane or moving vehicles. Thus, the locations of
the nodes follow a Gaussian distribution [ 22].
For deploying nodes, we propose a hybrid deployment
model based on a hybrid distribution that comprises a Gaussian
distribution (i.e., the normal distribution) and four uniform
distributions. The probability distribution function (PDF) of
the Gaussian distribution that deploys the sensor nodes in the
FoI is
f(x, y) = 1

We assume that BS is placed at the center of FoI, and so the
deployment point is set to the coordinates of BS. Rectangular
strips are deﬁned inside the FoI along its four outer edges,
as shown in Fig. 1. The PDF of the uniform distribution that

Our novel hybrid deployment model improves the coverage area in the FoI, and mitigates the energy-hole problem
when node density is high. Fig. 1 shows a sample deployment
in our simulation using our hybrid deployment method. The
Gaussian distribution model ensures extra nodes are deployed
